coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as ratiometric fluorescent probe for nitrite determination in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Xiang, Guoqiang
Wang, Yule
Zhang, Heng
Fan, Huanhuan
Fan, Lu
He, Lijun
Jiang, Xiuming
Zhao, Wenjie
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple and effective strategy for designing a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was described. A carbon dots (CDs) based dual-emission nanosensor for nitrite was prepared by coating the CDs on to dye-doped silica nanoparticles. Dual-emission silica nanoparticles fluorescence was quenched in sulfuric acid using potassium bromate (KBrO3). The nitrite present catalyzed the KBrO3 oxidation, resulting in ratiometric fluorescence response of the dual-emission silica nanoparticles. Several important parameters affecting the performance of the nanosensor were investigated. Under optimized conditions, the limit of detection was 1.0 ng mL−1 and the linear range 10–160 ng mL−1. Furthermore, the sensor was suitable for nitrite determination in different food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Nitrite
Silica nanoparticles
Food samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles
Characterization of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles
Sensing mechanism of the ratiometric nanosensor for NO2 −
Selection of acid media and concentration of the system
Effects of reaction temperature and time on catalyzing system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the preparation process of CDs-coated dual emission silica nanoparticles (RhB-Rhodamine B, APTES-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane, TEOS-Tetraethoxysilane).

The fluorescence spectra of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles solution (1.0 mg mL−1), KBrO3 (6.0 × 10−4 M), H2SO4 (0.01 M), reaction temperature 60 °C, reaction time 5 min and NO2 − concentrations vary from 10 to 180 ng mL−1.

Effect of acid type on the fluorescence quenching effect: CDs solution (1.0 mg mL−1), KBrO3 (6.0 × 10−4 M), NO2 − (40 ng mL−1), acid concentration (0.01 M), reaction temperature 60 °C, reaction time 5 min, excitation wavelength 360 nm.

Effect of reaction temperature and time on the fluorescence quenching effect: CDs solution (1.0 mg mL−1), NO2 − (40 ng mL−1), H2SO4 (0.01 M), reaction temperature varies from 40 to 80 °C, reaction time vary from 1 to 15 min, excitation wavelength 360 nm.
Determination of nitrite in different food samples (n = 3, mean ± SD).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as ratiometric fluorescent probe for nitrite determination in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple and effective strategy for designing a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was described. A carbon dots (CDs) based dual-emission nanosensor for nitrite was prepared by coating the CDs on to dye-doped silica nanoparticles. Dual-emission silica nanoparticles fluorescence was quenched in sulfuric acid using potassium bromate (KBrO3). The nitrite present catalyzed the KBrO3 oxidation, resulting in ratiometric fluorescence response of the dual-emission silica nanoparticles. Several important parameters affecting the performance of the nanosensor were investigated. Under optimized conditions, the limit of detection was 1.0 ng mL−1 and the linear range 10–160 ng mL−1. Furthermore, the sensor was suitable for nitrite determination in different food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is a chemically active substance that exists widely in the environment including food products. Nitrate and nitrite are used in combination with sodium chloride as antimicrobial agents in processed meats to inhibit the growth of bacterial spoilage. Examples include Clostridium botulinum, which generates botulin toxin that is responsible for muscular paralysis and neuronal complications (Cammack et al., 1999). Additionally, nitrite is widely used in food industries because of its ability to react with meat myoglobin to generate mononitrosylhaemochrome, which gives the characteristic red color of cured meat (Cammack et al., 1999). However, excess nitrite in foods is harmful, especially for pregnant women and infants (Burden, 1961; Canbay, Sahin, Kiran, & Akyilmaz, 2015; Seike et al., 2004; L. Wang et al., 2015). Thus, its presence in food has been limited by authorities in many regions globally. An acceptable daily intake of 0.06 mg (kg body weight)−1 of nitrite was established by the Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food additives in 2002 (FAO/WHO, 2003) (EU Directive). The World Health Organization (WHO) has set the maximum limit of nitrite in drinking water to 3.0 mg L−1 (or 65 μM) (WHO, 2004).

Overall, the quantitative determination of nitrite concentrations is of great importance, especially in food quality control (QC). Many methods for nitrite determination have been developed, including chromatographic techniques (Akyuz and Ata, 2009; Croitoru, 2012; Fang, Ohata, & Honda, 2009), electrochemical detection (Rajalakshmi et al., 2015; Turdean et al., 2015), spectrophotometry (Nagaraja, Al-Tayar, Shivakumar, Shrestha, & Gowda, 2010; Senra-Ferreiro, Pena-Pereira, Lavilla, & Bendicho, 2010; Tsikas, 2007; Zhang, Wang, Wang, Cui, & Fu, 1996), and spectrofluorimetric methods (Wang et al., 2015, 2016). Zhao, Zhao, and Li (2015) reviewed the analytical methods for nitrite based on electrochemical and spectroscopy. It was be pointed out that spectroscopic methodologies, especially spectrofluorimetry has lower limit of detection. Recently, several fluorescent probes for nitrite with good sensitivity and selectivity were reported (Biswas, Chowdhury, & Ray, 2004; Hong, Wei, Liang, Zhang, & Zhang, 2000; Kojima et al., 1999; Liu et al., 2009; Tsikas, 2007). Unfortunately, most of these probes are synthesized in the laboratory. Thus, they are either commercially unavailable or can only be purchased at high-cost. Thus, developing a highly sensitive and specific fluorescent probe for nitrite determination is still a major challenge.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been investigated as fluorophores for the sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal cations and anions due to their superior fluorescent properties, easy preparation, low-cost, and great potential for fluorescence-based analytical applications (Chaudhary, Kumar, Kaur, & Mehta, 2016; Du, Zeng, Ming, & Wu, 2013; Lu et al., 2017; C. Wang et al., 2016; Yang, Guo, Jia, & Zhang, 2017). Zhang’s group reported a new method for the selective and sensitive fluorescence detection of nitrite ions in water using nitrogen-doped CDs as a fluorophore (Zhang, Kang, Wang, Zhang, & Zhao, 2016). Published reports suggested that catalytic kinetics based on fluorimetry methods could be setup according to fluorescence quenching arising from the catalytic oxidation of fluorophores (Bu et al., 2008; Chen, Liu, & Gao, 2013; Feng and Liu, 2008; Zhu et al., 2014). However, so far, no reports demonstrated the utilization of CDs as fluorophores for the selective and sensitive detection of NO2 − in food samples based on the catalytic oxidation of fluorophores.

Herein and for the first time, we introduced the catalytic oxidation of CDs for the selective and sensitive detection of NO2 − in food samples. The CDs and Rhodamine B (RhB) were grafted on silica nanoparticles, and the two resulting fluorescence emission peaks were monitored. The fluorescence intensity was quenched by the oxidation effect of potassium bromate in H2SO4 media, which was catalyzed to yield an enhanced fluorescence quenching effect in presence of NO2 −. A sensitive ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor was accordingly developed based on the catalytic oxidation induced by the dual-emission silica nanoparticles. The selectivity and sensitivity of this ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor towards NO2 − were carefully investigated, and the feasibility of NO2 − determination in different food products was examined.

The fluorescence was measured using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was recorded using a JEM-2011 TEM. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in KBr were collected on a WQF-510 FT-IR spectrometer (Beijing Rayleigh Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China).

N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropylmethyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS), aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), Rhodamine B (RhB) and anhydrous citric acid (Analytical Reagent, AR) were purchased from Aladdin reagent company (Shanghai, China). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, AR) and KBrO3 (AR) were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China), and sodium nitrite (AR) from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China). Ethanol, NH4OH, H2SO4 and other reagents were purchased from Beijing chemicals (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water (18 MΩ cm−1) was used in all experiments.

The CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles were prepared according to the reported methods (Dong et al., 2012; Liu, Zhang, Bing, & Shangguan, 2014). The preparation procedure included three main steps, as shown in Fig. 1 . The organosilane functionalized CDs were first prepared by the pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid in AEAPMS at 230 °C for 1 min (Wang, Xie, Zhang, Liu, & Zhang, 2011). After purification by petroleum ether, the obtained product (0.4 mL) was added to 1.6 mL ethanol.

During this step, RhB was pre-coupled with APTES through the reaction between the carboxylic group of rhodamine and amino group of APTES in ethanol under constant stirring for 24 h at room temperature. The obtained RhB-APAES solution (100 μL), TEOS (50 μL) and ammonia (200 μL) were then added to ethanol (5 mL), and the mixture was left under constant stirring at room temperature for 8 h. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was centrifuged, and the silica nanoparticles were collected then washed three times with ethanol. Next, the obtained nanoparticles were ultrasonically dispersed in 5.0 mL ethanol for 10 min then 50 μL TEOS and 100 μL ammonia were added to the solution under constant stirring for 24 h at room temperature. The formed dye-doped silica nanoparticles were finally collected by centrifugation and washed three times with ethanol then redispersed in 5.0 mL ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: A 3.0 mL volume of dye-doped silica nanoparticles obtained above was mixed with 100 μL ammonia. After stirring for 30 min, a 10 μL CDs was added to the mixture and stirred for 15 h at room temperature. The reaction mixture was centrifuged, and the obtained CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles were washed three times with ethanol then redispersed in ethanol at concentration of 10 mg mL−1.

First, a 1.0 mL solution of CDs-coated silica nanoparticles (10 mg mL−1) was mixed with 50 μL H2SO4 (2.0 M) and 1.0 mL KBrO3 (6.0 × 10−3 M) in a 10 mL graduated tube. A certain amount of nitrite standard solution (or the sample solution) was added to the test tube. After diluting with ultrapure water to the required volume, the mixture was shaken thoroughly, and then left to rest for 5 min in a water bath at 60 °C prior to the fluorescence measurements. Simultaneously, a reagent blank was prepared without addition of the nitrite standard solution or sample solution.

The fluorescence intensities of the dual emission peaks (460 nm and 572 nm) for the test solution (F460 and F572 ) and the reagent blank (F0 460 and F0 572 ) were directly measured using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The spectrometric quantities were calculated according to: ΔF460 = F0 460 − F 460, ΔF572 = F0 572 − F572 , and the ΔF460/ΔF572 .

All the food products were purchased from a local supermarket in Zhengzhou (China), and meat samples (spiced beef and sliced dried beef) were triturated. Each sample (10 g, three parallel samples) was weighed and placed in a beaker, then 12.5 mL Na2B4O7·10H2O solution (5%) and 300 mL hot water were added. Next, the mixture was placed in a hot water bath (80–90 °C) for 15 min followed by cooling down to room temperature. It was then transferred to a volumetric flask, and 5 mL of potassium ferrocyanide with 10 mL zinc acetate aqueous solutions were added to the precipitated protein. The volume was adjusted to 500 mL followed by filtration through filter papers, and the resulting filtrate was stored at 4 °C.

The vegetable samples (Chinese leaves and radish peel) were cleaned and dried with a blower at room temperature then crushed before use. The vegetable samples were treated by the same procedures used above for meat samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles were prepared by the reaction between the amine and carboxyl groups. The success of the amidation reaction was confirmed by FT-IR of the reaction products, which showed typical signals of CONR vibration at 1654 cm−1 (CO) and 3456 cm−1 (NH). The peaks at 800 and 1100 cm−1 could be attributed to the SiO and SiCH2 stretching vibrations. These data clearly demonstrated the successful synthesis of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles. The TEM images revealed that the average diameter of the resulting CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles was ∼26 nm (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of the ratiometric nanosensor for NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of the CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles H2SO4-KBrO3-NO2 − are gathered in Fig. 2 . It will be noted that the CDs-coated silica nanoparticles showed two emission peaks (460 nm and 572 nm) at the single excitation wavelength of 360 nm. These two peaks were assigned to emissions induced by CDs (460 nm) and RhB (572 nm). Fig. 2 also depicted that the fluorescence signals of both the emission peaks were quenched slightly by the oxidation effect of KBrO3 in acidic media. However, great enhancements in the fluorescence quenching effect were observed when NO2 − was added to the system. Furthermore, the higher the NO2 − concentration, the greater the fluorescence quenching effect. This may be explained by the catalytic oxidation of CDs-coated silica nanoparticles by KBrO3 in presence of NO2 −. In other words, the oxidation of the dual-emission fluorescent nanoparticles by KBrO3 was accelerated in presence of NO2 −. The degree of acceleration (ΔF460 /ΔF572 ) showed a linear relationship with the concentration of NO2 −.

For the system containing 40 ng mL−1 NO2 −, the effects of nature and concentration of the acid, concentration of KBrO3, and the reaction temperature and time on ΔF of the system were investigated using an univariate approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of acid media and concentration of the system
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of acid type on ΔF of the system was investigated using different acids (HCl, H2SO4 and H3PO4), and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . It can clearly be observed that ΔF was much larger in H2SO4 than in HCl and H3PO4 media. This could be explained by the fact that H2SO4 has stronger acidity strength relative to other acids at equimolar concentrations, and KBrO3 showed strong oxidizing property in acidic media. Therefore, H2SO4 was selected as the reaction medium. The concentration of H2SO4 was also optimized, and the results indicated that 0.01 M H2SO4 yielded a maximum ΔF. Therefore, a 0.01 M H2SO4 concentration was selected as the reaction medium.

According to the sensing mechanism, the concentration of the oxidant (KBrO3) affects ΔF of the catalyzing system. Hence, KBrO3 is the sensitivity determining factor of the method based on the catalyzing system. The effect of KBrO3 was investigated by varying its concentration from 2.0 × 10−4 to 1.0 × 10−3 M, and the experimental results are gathered in Fig. S2. It will be noted that ΔF460 /ΔF572 of the catalyzing system first increased as the concentration of KBrO3 rose from 2.0 × 10−4 to 6.0 × 10−4 M, and then decreased as the concentration of KBrO3 further increased. Simultaneously, ΔF460 of the catalyzing system showed a similar trend as ΔF460/ΔF572 but ΔF572 of the catalyzing system depicted a slight change throughout the entire concentration range of KBrO3. Therefore, 6.0 × 10−4 M KBrO3 was selected as the optimal oxidant concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of reaction temperature and time on catalyzing system
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of both the reaction temperature and time on the catalyzing system were investigated. As can be observed in Fig. 4 , both ΔF460 and ΔF572 showed a similar trend when the reaction temperature was varied from 40 °C to 90 °C. They depicted an increase as the temperature rose followed by a significant decrease at higher temperatures up to 60 °C. This was probably induced by the catalysis effect, which was more efficient at higher temperatures (60 °C). On the other hand, the bare fluorescence intensities of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles sharply declined with a temperature higher than 60 °C, which, in turn, reduced ΔF460 and ΔF572. Therefore, 60 °C was selected as the optimal reaction temperature.

The reaction time was also investigated at the optimal reaction temperature according to the experimental methods. The results indicated that 5 min was sufficient to reach ΔF460 and ΔF572 maxima, and ΔF greatly declined for longer reaction periods. Thus, 5 min reaction time was selected as the optimal value and was used for further studies of the catalyzing system.

The effects of potential interferences from some metal ions, as well as other oxidants and reductants on the nitrite sensing system, were investigated. The solutions containing NO2 − (40 ng mL−1) and selected interferences were treated according to the recommended procedures under the optimum conditions. The tolerance limit of the coexisting materials, defined as the maximum concentration to achieve <5% variation in fluorescence intensities of both emission peaks are listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that most metals ions exhibited high tolerance limits but Br− revealed lower values. This could be explained by the fact that most of metal ions could not catalyze the KBrO3 oxidation, however, several highly charged ions (e.g. V(V), Cr(VI)) could also catalyze the oxidation of various organic dyes by bromate. (Cui, He, & Wang, 2006; Taylor et al., 1994); and Br- was also catalyzing the oxidation reaction by bromate in acidic media. Fortunately, elevated concentrations of Br-, V(V) and Cr(VI) are rare in most food samples for nitrite analysis.

Under the optimum conditions, the concentration of NO2 − showed a linear trend as a function of ΔF460/ΔF572 in the range of 10–160 ng mL−1. The linear regression equation was identified as: ΔF460/ΔF572 = 0.6375 + 0.0124C (ng mL−1), and the correlation coefficient (r) was 0.9971. The detection limit (3σ) of this method determined according to IUPAC was 1.0 ng mL−1 for NO2 −, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.2% (c = 40 ng mL−1, n = 7).

The proposed method was applied to determine NO2 − levels in leached solutions of food samples. The analytical results showed a good agreement with those obtained by standard methods based on GB 5009.33.2010 listed in Table S1 (According to student’s t-test, the calculated t values were lower than the referenced values with the confidence level of 95%). The comparative analytical data and recovery values of various food samples are gathered Table 2 . The recovery values were determined to be between 89.3% and 109.0%.

A sensitive ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor was developed to determine nitrite levels using the catalyzing effect of KBrO3 oxidation of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles. The catalyzed sensing system showed many advantages, including rapid detection, good selectivity, high sensitivity, and a wide linear response range. It also revealed potential applications for nitrite detection in various food samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China – China (Grant No. 21205028), Program for Science and Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (Grant No. 13HASTIT016), and Foundation for Young Core Teachers teacher of Henan University of Technology – China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2018.03.150.
